title: URL Encoder
shortDescription: Encode text into percent-escaped URL form for query strings, path segments, and form values.
metaDescription: Encode text into URL-safe form for query strings, path segments, and form values with precise percent-escaping.
intro: Paste text into the tool and it returns a URL-encoded string using percent escapes for reserved characters and spaces.
overview:
Use this tool when plain text must be embedded safely in a URL. It converts characters such as spaces, question marks, ampersands, slashes, and non-ASCII text into percent-encoded form so links and parameters stay valid.

This is especially useful when building query strings, preparing path segments, or passing user-entered text into APIs. The output preserves the original meaning while making the string safe for URL transport and parsing.
howItWorks: Enter the text you want to encode.
The tool converts reserved characters into percent-escaped bytes.
Copy the encoded result into a URL, parameter value, or request string.

useCases.title: Search query in a parameter
useCases.description: Turn a user search like café & tea into a safe parameter value for a results page or API request.

useCases.title: Prepping a redirect target
useCases.description: Encode a destination URL before placing it inside another URL so nested parameters are not broken.

useCases.title: Encoding file or page names
useCases.description: Convert spaces and punctuation in a path segment such as summer photos 2024 into URL-safe form.

useCases.title: Sending form text to an endpoint
useCases.description: Encode entered notes, tags, or labels before attaching them to a GET request or callback URL.

examples.title: Search query with symbols
examples.description: Spaces and reserved punctuation are escaped so the query can be safely inserted into a URL.

examples.title: Non-ASCII product name
examples.description: Accented characters are encoded as UTF-8 bytes before being percent-escaped.

examples.title: Path segment with slashes
examples.description: A slash inside a segment is encoded so it is not treated as a path separator.

faq.question: What characters get encoded first?
faq.answer: Reserved URL characters such as spaces, ?, &, =, #, /, and non-ASCII letters are encoded so they can be carried safely inside a URL.

faq.question: Why does a space become %20 instead of a plus sign?
faq.answer: %20 is the standard percent-encoded form for a space. A plus sign is mainly used in application/x-www-form-urlencoded form submissions, not in every URL context.

faq.question: Can I encode a whole URL here?
faq.answer: Yes, but be careful: encoding an entire URL also escapes separators like : and /. That is only correct when the full URL must be embedded inside another parameter value.

faq.question: Why do encoded results sometimes look longer?
faq.answer: Characters outside the safe URL set are expanded into percent-escaped bytes, so the output can be noticeably longer than the original text.

faq.question: What is the most common mistake?
faq.answer: Users often encode the entire address when they only need one parameter value. For normal links, encode only the piece that will be inserted into the URL.

seo.title: URL Encoder
seo.description: Encode text into URL-safe form for query strings, path segments, and form values with precise percent-escaping.
